highways.name: Staff Sergeant Michael A. Bock Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Allan Bense
honoree.summary: Allan Bense is an American politician and businessman from Florida. He was born on October 28, 1953, in Graceville, Florida. He served as a member of the Florida House of Representatives, representing the 5th district. Bense is also known for his involvement in the construction and real estate industries.
honoree.education: Florida State University, "Bachelors Degree"

highways.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Von Mizell
honoree.summary: Eula Johnson was an American civil rights activist known for her leadership in the desegregation of public facilities in Broward County, Florida. She was born on October 16, 1925, in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, and passed away on October 17, 2018. Johnson co-founded the Broward County Citizens for Equal Rights and played a pivotal role in the successful lawsuit to integrate Fort Lauderdale's beaches. Her activism significantly contributed to the broader civil rights movement in the United States.

highways.name: Sergeant Marvin L. Roberts Memorial Highway

honoree.name: CK Steele
honoree.summary: Charles Kenzie Steele was an American Baptist minister and civil rights leader born on July 2, 1925, in Tuskegee, Alabama. He played a pivotal role in the Tallahassee bus boycott of 1956, working alongside other leaders to challenge segregation. Steele served as the president of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) and was a key figure in the broader civil rights movement. He died on July 19, 1992, in Tallahassee, Florida, leaving a legacy of nonviolent activism.
honoree.education: Alabama State University, American Baptist Theological Seminary

honoree.name: A. Hagood Ellzey
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff Atticus Ellzey was shot and killed in Otter Creek after being lured into a wooded area by two men. Moments earlier Deputy Ellzey had asked the two white men to leave an African American hangout to avoid having any problems between the white and black citizens. As he entered the wooded area he was shot by the men. He was taken to a local doctor's office where he was able to identify the men who shot him. Both were arrested and sentenced to life in prison. Deputy Ellzey was survived by his wife and 14 children.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Trooper Patrick Ambroise
honoree.summary: Trooper Patrick Ambroise was killed when his patrol car was struck from behind while he was stopped on the shoulder of the Florida Turnpike, near Okeechobee Road. A vehicle being driven on the turnpike suddenly veered onto the shoulder and struck Trooper Ambroise's 2006 Crown Victoria from behind, causing it to burst into flames, and trapping him inside. The driver of the vehicle that caused the crash was seriously injured. Trooper Ambroise had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for four years. He is survived by his wife, two young daughters, mother, and five siblings.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

highways.name: Trooper John C. Hagerty Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper John C. Hagerty
honoree.summary: Trooper John Hagerty was killed when the traffic plane he was piloting was struck by a low-flying U.S. Navy plane over Lakeland. He was monitoring traffic on I-4, flying at an altitude of approximately 1,000 feet, when two Navy A7B Corsair bombers entered the airspace near him. One of the jets collided with his plane, slicing it in two. The wreckage of his plane landed on I-4, approximately four miles north of Lakeland. Trooper Hagerty and other troopers had previously reported Navy aircraft buzzing the traffic control planes and vehicles on the highway. Trooper Hagerty had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 13 years.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Aircraft accident

highways.name: Trooper Joseph Sawtell Jr. Memorial Highway

Sergeant George A. Brown, III Memorial Highway
Trooper Bradley S. Glascock Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Bradley S. Glascock
honoree.summary: Trooper Bradley Glascock was shot and killed when he made a traffic stop in Miami on a driver for failing to pay a ten-cent toll on the Dolphin Expressway (Route 836). The trooper and the civilian "ride-along", a 20-year-old college freshman, observed a vehicle stop several feet before the toll booth on the 836 Expressway. After Trooper Glascock pulled up behind the car, the driver pulled through the toll booth without paying. Trooper Glascock stopped the vehicle, and as he was getting out, the suspect began backing up. Trooper Glascock backed his vehicle up to avoid a collision. This happened several times, and at that point, Trooper Glascock told his ride-along that he intended to arrest the suspect. Trooper Glascock and the suspect both exited their vehicles and exchanged words. The suspect then fled back to his vehicle, where he retrieved a .38-caliber handgun and shot Trooper Glascock twice, killing him. The suspect then entered his vehicle. The ride-along unlocked the 12-gauge shotgun that was mounted in the patrol car, loaded it, and opened fire at the suspect. The suspect sped away, but not before the ride-along was able to fire four shots, wounding him. The ride-along then radioed for help. Trooper Glascock was transported to the hospital where he was pronounced dead. A massive manhunt ensued. Working with the description that the ride-along provided, along with a partial license plate of the suspect's vehicle, the police were able to identify the suspect. During the investigation, the suspect’s vehicle was recovered along with the 38-caliber Titen revolver used to kill the trooper. Roadblocks were set up all over Miami, and after two days, the suspect turned himself in because he was unable to get out of town. The 31-year-old suspect pled guilty to first-degree murder and was sentenced to life in prison. He was denied parole in 2011 and again in 2017. As of November 23, 2020, he was in prison. Trooper Glascock's murder spurred the state cabinet to buy bullet-resistant vests for all state troopers. Trooper Glascock had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for one year. He was survived by his parents, brother, and sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun; .38 caliber Offender: Sentenced to life

highways.name: Trooper Lindell J. Gibbons Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Lindell J. Gibbons
honoree.summary: Trooper Lindell Gibbons was killed in an automobile crash on Interstate 75 and County Road 951 (current-day Collier Boulevard) in Collier County at 4:30 p.m. While attempting to turn around at 4:30 p.m., he was struck by a tractor-trailer, killing him instantly. The driver of the tractor-trailer was not charged. Trooper Gibbons was a United States Air Force Vietnam War veteran and served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 2 years. He previously served as a law enforcement officer in Missouri. He was survived by his wife and two sons.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

honoree.name: Cleo L. Tomlinson
honoree.summary: Corporal Cleo Tomlinson, Trooper Robert Pruitt, and Trooper Merle Cook were killed in an airplane accident while searching for two break-and-enter suspects in St. John's County. The suspects were charged with three counts of homicide in connection with the deaths of all three troopers. The primary burglary suspect was convicted of burglary and sentenced to 15 years in prison.  He was released only to be sentenced to 6 years for cocaine sale in October of 2010 and he currently resides in the Florida State Prison System. Corporal Tomlinson had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 19 years.

honoree.summary: Trooper Strickland was killed in an automobile accident while pursuing a vehicle in Titusville. He swerved to avoid another vehicle and struck a tree. Trooper Strickland had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 7 years.

Sergeant John C. Baxter, Jr. Memorial Highway

honoree.name: John C. Baxter
honoree.summary: I worked with John.  He was one the nicest people I have ever met.  He never had a bad word to say about anyone and was a joy to work with.  As a young Trooper at the time, every time John flew in the county I was assigned to, he would call me and invite me to lunch, ask how I was and if I was having any problems.  He always listened to me so patiently.   I was on duty the day he died.  I am not ashamed to admit I sat on I-75 and just cried.  Its been 35 years since that awful awful day.  I still see that smile, still hear that laugh and miss him.  The FHP, the State of Florida and its citizens lost someone special that day.  I will never forget him telling me... Son, when you write a man a ticket, you take bread from the mouth of his child.  Respect that and him because its no small thing. Trooper G. Hamilton FHP March 2nd, 2021

honoree.name: Trooper Wilburn A. Kelly
honoree.summary: Trooper Wilburn Kelly was killed in an automobile crash when he was struck from behind while on patrol in St. Augustine. Trooper Kelly had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 22 years. He was survived by his wife and two children.

highways.name: Trooper Edwin J. Gasque Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Edwin J. Gasque
honoree.summary: Trooper Edwin Gasque was shot and killed when he was ambushed while he and his police dog tracked two fugitives in Jacksonville. The suspects were wanted for abducting and shooting at a Duval County sheriff's deputy. Both suspects were captured one week later near Dawson, Georgia. Both were convicted of first degree murder and sentenced to life in prison on June 18, 1962. One died in prison on July 14, 1987, and the second died in prison on June 20, 2012. Trooper Gasque was a U.S. Army veteran and had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for two years. He was survived by his wife and two children.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Rifle; .22 caliber Offender: Died in prison

Trooper Alvin V. Kohler Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Alvin V. Kohler
honoree.summary: Trooper Alvin Kohler was shot and killed in Miami when he stopped to help a disabled motorist on the Florida Turnpike at SW 168th Street. Trooper Kohler stopped for a car on fire. He requested a tow truck and started writing the 15-year-old driver a citation for failure to comply with a restriction on his license. When the tow truck arrived, the youth opened fire with a handgun, killing the trooper and the tow truck driver. The suspect and a 13-year-old companion then fled in the tow truck. Minutes later, they were captured by two Florida City police officers. The shooter was convicted of two counts of murder and robbery and sentenced to three life terms in prison. As of November 22, 2020, he was in prison. Trooper Kohler was a U.S. Air Force veteran and had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 11 months. He was survived by his parents, two brothers, and a sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun; .357 caliber Offender: Sentenced to life

highways.name: Trooper Milan D. Hendrix Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Milan D. Hendrix
honoree.summary: Trooper Milan Hendrix was killed in an automobile crash in Pensacola when his patrol car collided with a log truck exiting a paper mill on U.S. 29 at 10:00 p.m. Trooper Hendrix had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for nine years. He was survived by his parents, brother, and sister. In December 2019, Highway 29 was renamed Trooper Milan Hendrix Memorial Highway, from Nine Mile Road to Neal Road.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

honoree.name: Officer Lance Christian Whitaker
honoree.summary: Police Officer Lance Whitaker was killed in a single-vehicle crash on northbound I-295, near Alta Drive, at approximately 4:30 a.m. He was responding to another accident scene on the interstate when his vehicle left the roadway and struck a tree. Officer Whitaker served more than 17 years with the Jacksonville Sheriff’s Office. He is survived by his teenage son, fiancee and her three children. In December 2019, I-295 between Pulaski and Alta was renamed to Officer Lance Christian Waller Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

highways.name: Deputy Sheriff David Anthony Abella Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Sheriff David Anthony Abella
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff David Abella was killed in an automobile collision on 50th Street, near Causeway Boulevard while responding to a trespassing call at a local convenience store. He was attempting to pass a slow-moving tractor-trailer when he lost control of his patrol car and struck a utility pole. The deputy was transported to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead. Deputy Abella was a United States Coast Guard Persian Gulf veteran and had served with the Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office for one year. He is survived by his wife, three children, parents, brother, and sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

honoree.name: Harriet Tubman
honoree.summary: Harriet Tubman was born into slavery in Maryland around 1822 and escaped to freedom in 1849. She became a famous conductor on the Underground Railroad, leading dozens of enslaved people to freedom and earning the nickname 'Moses.' During the American Civil War, she served the Union Army as a scout, spy, and nurse. After the war, she was a prominent activist for women's suffrage and civil rights.

honoree.name: Joshua S. Montaad
honoree.summary: Officer Joshua Montaad was killed in a single-vehicle crash on U.S. 19, near Burley Brannen Road, in Taylor County. His patrol car left the roadway and struck a tree during a period of heavy rain. The vehicle caught fire and became engulfed in flames. Officer Montaad had served with the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services - Office of Agricultural Law Enforcement for two years.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

honoree.name: Wesley L. Silas
honoree.summary: Officer Wesley Silas was killed after being struck by a tractor-trailer at the Agricultural Inspection Station on I-10 near Live Oak, Florida. He had stopped the truck for inspection and had just completed his paperwork, noting several suspicious items on the truck's registration. After returning the registration to the driver he walked in front of the truck to return to the inspection station when the driver began to drive forward, striking Officer Silas. The driver claimed he did not see Officer Silas walk in front of the truck. Due to a lack of evidence, no charges were ever filed against the trucker, and Officer Silas' death was ruled accidental. Officer Silas had served with the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services - Office of Agricultural Law Enforcement for 12 years. He was survived by his mother, sister, and brother.

honoree.name: Harriet Tubman
honoree.summary: Harriet Tubman was born into slavery in Maryland around 1822 and escaped to freedom in 1849. She became a famous conductor on the Underground Railroad, leading dozens of enslaved people to freedom and earning the nickname 'Moses.' During the American Civil War, she served the Union Army as a scout, spy, and nurse. After the war, she was a prominent activist for women's suffrage and civil rights.

honoree.name: Officer James Ronco
honoree.summary: Officer Ronco was shot and killed by a female prisoner who had just escaped from the police station. While he was transporting her back to jail she grabbed his gun and shot him twice. He was able to regain control of the weapon and shot and killed the suspect as well. It was determined that the suspect was under the influence of heroin and cocaine.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Officer's handgun Offender: Shot and killed

highways.name: Corporal Joseph R. Bertrand Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Corporal Joseph R. Bertrand
honoree.summary: Corporal Bertrand was shot and killed on State Road 80 near Ft. Myers when he stopped a driver he suspected was under the influence. The suspect was captured in a nearby wooded area. The suspect was convicted of first degree murder and sentenced to death May 17, 1968. His conviction and sentence were later reduced to second degree murder with a life sentence. He died in prison on May 12, 2000. Trooper Bertrand had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 16 years.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Gun; Unknown type Offender: Died in prison

Trooper James Herbert Fulford, Jr., Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper James Herbert Fulford, Jr
honoree.summary: Trooper James Fulford was killed by a bomb while searching a vehicle. One suspect made the bomb, placed it inside a microwave, and gift wrapped it. He then paid a second male to deliver the package to his ex-wife, who had prior knowledge of a murder he had been involved with and had intimate knowledge of an active drug ring he was involved with. The male agreed to deliver the package thinking it only contained drugs. Trooper Fulford pulled over the car driven by a male and one of his associates. Both men were arrested during the traffic stop and taken to jail by two Jefferson County deputies. Shortly thereafter, Trooper Fulford was killed when the bomb exploded while searching the car. An explosive expert later determined that Trooper Fulford was holding the package when it exploded. Trooper Fulford's death prompted a state and federal investigation that broke apart a drug ring and led to the indictment of 28 people. The bomb maker was sentenced to death and was subsequently executed on February 26th, 2014. The male subject driving the vehicle was sentenced to 40 years. In addition, the brother of the bomb-maker was sentenced to life for first-degree murder. Trooper Fulford had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 14 years. He is survived by his wife and two children. In 2014, the portion of I-10 between the Aucilla and Monticello exits was dedicated the "Trooper James Herbert Fulford Jr. Memorial Highway."
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Bomb Weapon: Explosives; Bomb Offender: Executed in 2014

honoree.name: Carl Mertes
honoree.summary: Police Officer Carl Mertes was shot and killed while backing up another officer who was making an arrest. Officer Mertes was on patrol and responded to backup another officer at a nearby park at 3 a.m. When he arrived the suspect fled and Officer Mertes pursued him. He caught the suspect and as he handcuffed him the man produced a handgun and shot him in the neck and under his arm, causing him to bleed to death. Although mortally wounded, Officer Mertes was able to return fire and wound the suspect, who was apprehended in the park later in the night. The 32-year-old subject was convicted of second degree murder and sentenced to life in prison. He died in prison on April 25, 2019. The park in which Officer Mertes was killed was renamed in his honor, to Carl W. Mertes Park. Officer Mertes had been with the agency for ten years and was survived by his wife, children, and step-children.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun Offender: Died in prison

honoree.name: Larcenia Bullard
honoree.summary: Larcenia Bullard was an American politician who served as a member of the Florida Senate. She was born on December 14, 1942, in Mullins, South Carolina. Bullard represented the 39th district and was known for her advocacy in education and healthcare. She passed away on December 14, 2023, leaving a legacy of public service in Florida politics.
honoree.education: University of Miami, Florida International University

highways.name: Indian Key Irving R. Eyster Bridge
RADM LeRoy Collins, Jr., Veterans Expressway

honoree.name: C. Bette Wimbish
honoree.summary: C. Bette Wimbish was an American lawyer and civil rights activist who became the first African American woman to pass the Florida bar exam. Born in 1932, she worked as a public defender and later served as an assistant state attorney. She was a prominent figure in the fight for racial and gender equality in the legal profession. She died in 2008, leaving a legacy as a trailblazer in Florida's legal system.
honoree.education: Florida A&M University, Boston University School of Law

honoree.name: Albert W. Gilchrist
honoree.summary: Albert William Gilchrist was an American politician who served as the 18th Governor of Florida from 1909 to 1913. He was born on January 15, 1858, in South Carolina and died on May 15, 1926, in Fort Myers, Florida. Gilchrist was a member of the Democratic Party and also had a career as a teacher and a military officer. His historical significance lies in his governance during the Progressive Era and his legacy in Florida's development.
honoree.education: University of South Carolina, United States Military Academy Preparatory School

honoree.name: S. Chesterfield Smith
honoree.summary: Chesterfield Smith was an American lawyer who served as the President of the American Bar Association. He was born on August 23, 1930, in Lakeland, Florida, and died on December 25, 2003, in Tallahassee, Florida. Smith was a prominent figure in the legal profession, known for his leadership and advocacy for judicial independence. His legacy is commemorated through the Chesterfield Smith Award, which honors excellence in legal practice.
honoree.education: University of Florida, Fredric G. Levin College of Law

highways.name: Trooper Jeffery Dale Young Memorial Bridge

honoree.name: Trooper Jeffery Dale Young
honoree.summary: Trooper Jeffrey Young was shot and killed with his own weapon during a traffic stop in Manatee, Florida. Trooper Young was a member of the narcotics squad and was attempting to arrest a drug courier during a traffic stop. Two suspects were en route to Detroit, Michigan, on I-75 when they were stopped by Trooper Young.  Consent was given to search the vehicle, during which Trooper Young discovered a substantial amount of cocaine.  The amount was later determined to be 340 grams of crack cocaine. A violent struggle between the Trooper and suspects ensued, and one of the suspects gained control of Trooper Young's revolver and shot him in the face at point-blank range. The 42-year-old shooter and his 40-year-old accomplice were apprehended three hours later in a nearby swamp. The shooter was convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to death. As of November 28, 2020, now 75, he remains on death row. Trooper Young served with the Florida Highway Patrol for three years and was assigned to Troop F. He was well known for his ability to spot a drug trafficker. He was survived by his wife and daughter.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Officer's handgun Offender: Sentenced to death

honoree.name: Claude Pepper
honoree.summary: Claude Pepper was an American politician born on September 8, 1900, in Lee County, Alabama. He served as a U.S. Senator and Congressman from Florida, becoming a prominent advocate for civil rights and senior citizens. Known as 'Mr. Sunshine,' he was a leading voice against age discrimination and helped pass the Age Discrimination in Employment Act. He died on May 30, 1989, in Bethesda, Maryland, leaving a legacy as a champion for the elderly.
honoree.education: University of Alabama, Harvard Law School

Tanzler was also a member of the Florida House of Representatives and later served as a United States Attorney. He died on January 13, 2022, in Jacksonville, Florida.

He was born on January 1, 1913, in St. Augustine, Florida, and died on January 1, 1996, in the same city. His political career was primarily focused on representing Florida's interests in the state legislature. He is remembered for his contributions to local governance and public service in Florida.

honoree.name: Correction Officers
honoree.summary: Corrections Officer Brad McNew was shot and killed at the Love's Travel Stop at 12921 Duval Road in Jacksonville after intervening in a domestic altercation. At 1:20 a.m., Officer McNew stopped at the convenience store and witnessed a man assaulting a female. When he tried to help the woman, the man aimed a gun at him before returning to his car. The subject leaned out of his car window and shot Officer McNew before driving away. Officer McNew was transported to a local hospital where he died from his wounds. The suspect was arrested in North Carolina the following day. He is charged with murder, possession of a firearm by a convicted felon, and aggravated assault with a firearm. Officer McNew had served with the Jacksonville Sheriff's Office for 24 years. He is survived by his wife and son.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Gun; Unknown type Offender: In custody

highways.name: Deputy Hal P. Croft and Deputy Ronald Jackson Highway
Deputy Renee Danell Azure Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Renee Danell Azure
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff Renee Azure was killed in an automobile crash while responding to a burglar alarm in Raiford at 12:20 am. During the response, her patrol car went out of control on SR-121 just north of Lake Butler and struck several trees. Deputy Azure was thrown from the patrol car and died at the scene. Deputy Azure had served with the Union County Sheriff's Office for five months, was their first female deputy, and had been in law enforcement for two years. She is survived by her 3-year-old daughter, parents, and sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

highways.name: Lieutenant Colonel Carl John Luksic, USAF, Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Robert L. F. Sikes
honoree.summary: Bob Sikes was an American politician who served as a U.S. Representative for Florida. He was born on January 3, 1908, in Graceville, Florida, and had a long career in public service. He served in Congress for several decades, with a hiatus during World War II when he served in the U.S. Army Air Forces. He died on September 13, 1994, leaving a legacy as a conservative Democrat and a key figure in Florida's political history.
honoree.education: Rollins College, Stetson University

honoree.name: Senator Greg Evers
honoree.summary: Greg Evers was an American politician born on July 1, 1964, in Pensacola, Florida. He served as a member of the Florida House of Representatives and later the Florida Senate, representing districts in the Florida Panhandle. Evers was a Republican known for his work on education and environmental issues in the state legislature. He died in a car accident on August 21, 2020, in Okaloosa County, Florida.

honoree.name: Air Commando
honoree.summary: John R. Alison was a highly decorated American fighter pilot and Air Force officer born on June 11, 1912. He served with distinction in World War II, becoming an ace and a leading figure in the China-Burma-India theater. He later played a pivotal role in the formation of the U.S. Air Force Special Operations Command as a founder of the Air Commandos. He passed away on June 6, 2011, leaving a legacy as a military innovator and hero.
honoree.education: United States Military Academy

highways.name: Brigadier General Thomas 'Mark' Stogsdill Memorial Overpass

honoree.name: Danny Wuerffel
honoree.summary: Danny Wuerffel is a former American football quarterback who won the Heisman Trophy and a national championship with the University of Florida. Born on May 4, 1974, he played professionally in the NFL and other leagues before transitioning to a career in ministry and public speaking. He is recognized for his athletic achievements and subsequent community leadership. His legacy includes both his on-field success and his off-field contributions to society.
honoree.education: University of Florida
honoree.placeOfBirth: Fort Walton Beach, Florida, United States

honoree.name: Whiting Field
honoree.summary: Eugene Cernan was an American astronaut born on March 14, 1934, in Chicago, Illinois. He was the commander of Apollo 17 and became the last person to walk on the Moon in December 1972. A naval aviator and engineer, he flew on three space missions including Gemini 9A and Apollo 10. He passed away on January 16, 2017, leaving a legacy as a pioneer of space exploration.
honoree.education: Purdue University, Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering, United States Naval Postgraduate School, Master of Science in Aeronautical Engineering

honoree.name: E. Clay Shaw, Jr
honoree.summary: Clay Shaw was an American politician and lawyer born on October 16, 1939, in Fort Lauderdale, Florida. He served as a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from 1981 to 2007, representing Florida's 22nd congressional district. Known for his work on tax policy and Social Security reform, he was a prominent figure in Congress for over two decades. He passed away on September 10, 2013, in Fort Lauderdale, leaving a legacy of bipartisan legislative achievements.
honoree.education: University of Florida, Stetson University College of Law

honoree.name: Trooper Donald Earl Jennings
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Struck by vehicle

highways.name: Trooper Stephen G. Rouse Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Stephen G. Rouse
honoree.summary: Trooper Stephen Rouse was killed in an automobile crash while responding to another accident call on Alligator Alley. As he passed through a toll plaza a van made a U-turn in front of him, causing him to collide with it. Trooper Rouse had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 11 months. He was survived by his wife, parents, and sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Automobile crash

honoree.name: Richard Raczkoski
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff Richard Raczkoski was shot and killed at 3 a.m. while checking on a suspicious person using a pay phone in front of a closed convenience store at 8200 20th Street in Vero Beach. While waiting for a cab to pick the suspect up, a handgun fell from his pants. The suspect picked up the handgun and shot Deputy Raczkoski five times in the back as the deputy attempted to take cover behind his patrol car. The suspect was apprehended in Albany two days later. An ex-convict who had been out of prison for five months, the suspect was convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to death. Deputy Raczkoski had served with the Indian River County Sheriff's Office for over three years. He was survived by his parents, brother, and fiancée.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun; .380 caliber Offender: Sentenced to death

honoree.name: Dan McCarty
honoree.summary: Daniel T. McCarty was an American politician who served as the 36th Governor of Florida from 1953 until his death in 1954. He was born on January 6, 1912, in St. Petersburg, Florida. McCarty was a member of the Democratic Party and had a career in public service before his gubernatorial tenure. He died on December 13, 1954, in Tallahassee, Florida, leaving a legacy as a progressive leader in post-war Florida.
honoree.education: St. Petersburg Junior College, University of Florida

honoree.name: Jerry Thomas
honoree.summary: Jerry Thomas was an American politician from Florida whose career and life details are not widely documented in public sources. No specific birth or death dates are available for him in the provided content. His professional activities appear to be limited to politics within the state of Florida. Historical significance and notable achievements could not be determined from the available information.

honoree.name: Wayne Koester
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff Wayne Koester and two other deputies were shot while responding to a domestic violence call at a home on Hilda Avenue in the Ocala National Forest. The three deputies had made contact with the female subject at a neighbor's home. The deputies searched for the male subject at the couple's home but did not locate him. As they returned the female subject to the home, the man ambushed them from the woods with a shotgun loaded with slugs. He exchanged shots with the deputies, wounding all three. Deputy Koester was transported to a local hospital where he succumbed to his wounds. The man fled the scene on a motorcycle but was apprehended later in the day following a massive manhunt and a second shootout. When the suspect was apprehended, it was discovered that he suffered gunshot wounds from one of the shootouts. He was convicted of first-degree murder, two counts of attempted first-degree murder of a law enforcement officer, and two counts of aggravated battery on a law enforcement officer. He was subsequently sentenced to death. During a second trial in 2022, he was sentenced to life in prison. Deputy Koester was a member of the Florida Army National Guard who had served with the Lake County Sheriff's Office for only nine months and had previously served with the Umatilla Police Department for eight years. He is survived by his wife and four children.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Shotgun Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Rosa Parks
honoree.summary: Rosa Louise McCauley Parks was an American activist known as the 'first lady of civil rights'. She was born on February 4, 1913, in Tuskegee, Alabama. On December 1, 1955, in Montgomery, Alabama, she refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger, leading to the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Her actions were pivotal in the movement to end racial segregation in the United States. She died on October 24, 2005, in Detroit, Michigan.
honoree.education: Alabama State Teachers College for Negroes, Highlander Folk School

highways.name: Edward Daniel Davis, Sr., Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Martin L. King
honoree.summary: Martin Luther King Jr. was born on January 15, 1929, in Atlanta, Georgia, and became the most prominent leader of the American Civil Rights Movement. He advocated for nonviolent civil disobedience to combat racial injustice and delivered his iconic 'I Have a Dream' speech during the 1963 March on Washington. King was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964 for his efforts to end racial segregation and discrimination. He was assassinated on April 4, 1968, in Memphis, Tennessee, leaving a lasting legacy as a symbol of the fight for equality.
honoree.education: Morehouse College, Crozer Theological Seminary, Boston University

Senator N. Ray Carroll Memorial Interchange

honoree.name: Hamilton Disston
honoree.summary: Hamilton Disston was an American industrialist and land developer born on August 23, 1841, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. He was a prominent saw manufacturer who expanded his family's tool business into a major industrial enterprise. Disston is historically significant for his massive land purchases in Florida, which played a key role in the state's post-Civil War economic development. He died on April 30, 1896, in Philadelphia.

honoree.name: William A. Kratzert
honoree.summary: Billy Kratzert is a retired American professional golfer born on October 2, 1946, in Charlotte, North Carolina. He won four PGA Tour events during his career, with his first victory coming at the 1974 Kemper Open. Kratzert is also known for his work as a golf commentator for Golf Channel and ESPN. He was inducted into the Georgia Sports Hall of Fame in 2008.

St. Johns River Veterans Memorial Bridge

honoree.name: Ambassador Armando Valladares
honoree.summary: Armando Valladares is a Cuban poet, novelist, and political activist known for his opposition to the Castro regime. He was arrested in 1960 at age 22 and spent 22 years in Cuban prisons before his release in 1982. After his release, he became an author and served as the United States Ambassador to the United Nations Human Rights Commission from 2002 to 2004. His memoir 'Against All Hope' details his experiences as a political prisoner.

honoree.name: American Bankers
honoree.summary: Dana A. Dorsey was an African-American businessman, carpenter, and philanthropist born in the late 19th century. He became one of the wealthiest Black citizens in Florida during the early 20th century through his real estate and lumber businesses. Dorsey is best known for founding the Dorsey Memorial Library, the first library for African Americans in Miami-Dade County. His legacy includes significant contributions to education and community development in Miami, Florida.

Angel Pio de la Portilla Way

honoree.name: Arthur Mays
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Handgun Offender: Shot and killed

honoree.name: Athalie Range
honoree.summary: M. Athalie Range was an American civil rights activist and politician born on July 2, 1910, in Miami, Florida. She became the first African American woman elected to the Miami City Commission in 1970. Her activism focused on improving education and housing for African Americans in Miami. She died on November 23, 1989, in Miami, leaving a legacy of community service and political breakthroughs.

honoree.name: Benjamin Leon
honoree.summary: Benjamin Chaires was a prominent American pioneer, entrepreneur, and landowner in early 19th-century Florida. He was born in 1773 in Maryland and died in 1837 in Leon County, Florida. Chaires was a significant figure in the development of the Florida territory, particularly through his involvement in banking, railroads, and land speculation. His legacy includes substantial contributions to the economic and infrastructural growth of the region during its formative years.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Motorcycle crash Incident Date: Sunday, August 19, 2018

highways.name: Captains Padron, Perez and Sosa Way

honoree.name: Carrie P. Meek
honoree.summary: Carrie Meek was an American politician and educator who served as a U.S. Representative for Florida's 17th congressional district from 1993 to 2003. Born on September 29, 1926, in Tallahassee, Florida, she was the first African American woman elected to represent Florida in Congress. Prior to her political career, she was a notable academic administrator at Miami Dade College. She passed away on November 28, 2021, in Miami, Florida, leaving a legacy of advocacy for education and civil rights.
honoree.education: Florida A&M University, University of Miami

highways.name: Cuban‐American Association of Civil Engineers Way

honoree.name: Oscar Elias Biscet
honoree.summary: Óscar Elías Biscet is a Cuban physician, political dissident, and human rights activist known for his non-violent opposition to the Cuban government. He was born on July 20, 1961, in Havana, Cuba, and has faced multiple arrests and imprisonment for his activism. Biscet is the founder of the Lawton Foundation for Human Rights and has received international recognition for his advocacy. He was released from prison in 2011 and continues to be a prominent figure in the Cuban opposition movement.

honoree.name: Father Gerard Jean‐Juste
honoree.summary: Gérard Jean-Juste was a Haitian Roman Catholic priest and activist born on February 7, 1946. He served as a pastor in Miami and became a prominent advocate for Haitian refugees' rights. Jean-Juste was arrested in Haiti in 2005 for opposing the interim government but was later released due to international pressure. He died on May 27, 2009, in Port-au-Prince, leaving a legacy of humanitarian work and social justice advocacy.

highways.name: Historic Biscayne Boulevard: Gateway to Miami
Honorable Robert B. Ingram, Ph.D., Boulevard

honoree.name: Jean‐Jacques Dessalines
honoree.summary: Jean-Jacques Dessalines was born on September 20, 1758, in Grande-Rivière-de-Nippes, Saint-Domingue. He was a key leader of the Haitian Revolution and became the first ruler of an independent Haiti. As Emperor, he abolished slavery and declared the nation's sovereignty in 1804. He was assassinated on October 17, 1806, in Port-au-Prince, Haiti.

highways.name: Jean Baptiste Point du Sable Boulevard
Judge Wilkie D. Ferguson, Jr. Boulevard

honoree.name: Julia Tuttle
honoree.summary: Julia Tuttle was an American businesswoman and real estate investor born in 1849. She is known as the only woman to have founded a major American city, Miami, Florida. Tuttle moved to South Florida in the 1890s and orchestrated the land deal that led to the city's incorporation in 1896. She died in 1898, leaving a lasting legacy as the 'Mother of Miami.'

honoree.name: Lolo Villalobos
honoree.summary: J. Alex Villalobos is a former American politician who served in the Florida Senate. He was born on June 20, 1964, in Miami, Florida, and is still alive as of the provided information. His career was primarily focused on public service and legislative work in Florida. He is recognized for his contributions to state-level politics.
honoree.education: University of Florida

Mayor General Ignacio Agramonte y Loynaz Roadway

honoree.name: Orange Bowl
honoree.summary: Anita Bryant is an American singer and former Miss Oklahoma born on September 25, 1940, in Barnsdall, Oklahoma. She gained fame as a popular singer in the 1960s and later became a spokesperson for the Florida Citrus Commission. Bryant is historically significant for her leadership in the anti-gay rights movement in the late 1970s, particularly through her 'Save Our Children' campaign. She faced significant backlash and career decline as a result of her activism, though she remains a notable cultural figure.

highways.name: Pastors Dr. Randall & Sharlene Holts Street
Reverend Dr. C.P. Preston, Jr. Street

honoree.name: Saint Marcellin Champagnat
honoree.summary: Marcellin Joseph Benedict Champagnat was born on May 20, 1789, in the Kingdom of France. He was a French Catholic priest and educator who founded the Marist Brothers, a religious congregation dedicated to teaching youth. Champagnat was beatified in 1955 and canonized as a saint in 1999 by Pope John Paul II. He is historically significant for establishing a global network of schools and promoting education for all children.
honoree.education: Seminary of Saint-Irénée, "Minor Seminary of LArgentière"

honoree.name: Sunny Isles
honoree.summary: Carl Icahn is an American businessman and investor known for his role as a corporate raider and activist shareholder. Born on February 16, 1936, in Brooklyn, New York, he built a fortune through takeovers and restructuring of companies. He is the founder of Icahn Enterprises and has been involved in numerous high-profile corporate battles. His career has made him one of the most prominent and controversial figures in finance.
honoree.education: Princeton University, New York University School of Law

honoree.name: Dante B. Fascell
honoree.summary: Dante Fascell was an American politician born on July 9, 1917, in New York City. He served as a Democratic member of the U.S. House of Representatives for Florida's 4th congressional district. Fascell was a key figure in foreign policy and co-founded the House Select Committee on Aging. He died on December 28, 1998, in Bethesda, Maryland, leaving a legacy of bipartisan legislative work.

honoree.name: Representative Bernie C. Papy
honoree.summary: Bernie Papy is an American former professional basketball player and coach. He played college basketball for the University of Florida and later professionally in Europe. Papy served as an assistant coach for the Florida Gators men's basketball team under head coach Norm Sloan. He is also known for his involvement in local politics and community service in Florida.
honoree.education: University of Florida

honoree.name: Harry S. Truman Overseas
honoree.summary: Harry S. Truman was the 33rd President of the United States, serving from 1945 to 1953. He was born on May 8, 1884, in Lamar, Missouri, and died on December 26, 1972, in Kansas City, Missouri. Truman led the country through the end of World War II and the early Cold War, making the decision to use atomic bombs on Japan. He is historically significant for establishing the Truman Doctrine and the Marshall Plan to contain communism.
honoree.education: Independence High School, University of Missouri, Kansas City Law School

honoree.name: Lonnie Coburn
honoree.summary: Deputy Lonnie Coburn was shot and killed when he confronted two suspects wanted in the connection of the rape, kidnapping, and murder of a young woman who was six months pregnant. The two attacked him, gained control of his service weapon, and shot him twice. Both suspects were eventually apprehended, convicted of both murders, and sentenced to death. Both, 32 and 22, later had their sentences commuted to life. As on November 23, 2020, both were in prison. A portion of State Road 50 near the scene of his murder was dedicated in his memory.

honoree.name: Trooper Kenneth E. Flynt
honoree.summary: Trooper Kenneth Flynt was shot and killed while attempting to stop a robbery at 508 South Florida Circle in Apollo Beach while off duty. An armed robber attempted to steal a vehicle from a neighbor of Trooper Flynt. The victim ran to Trooper Flynt's home for help, with the suspect chasing her. As Trooper Flynt tried to take action, he was shot and killed. The suspect was convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to death on May 14, 1976. On March 17, 1977, he was found dead from a drug overdose in his cell at the Florida Penitentiary. Trooper Flynt had served with the Florida Highway Patrol for 19 years. He was survived by his wife and two daughters.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Gun; Unknown type Offender: Died in prison

highways.name: Deputy John C. Mecklenburg Memorial Highway

honoree.name: John C. Mecklenburg
honoree.summary: Deputy John Mecklenburg was killed in an automobile accident in Pasco County while involved in a vehicle pursuit. Officers from the Brooksville Police Department had started pursuing a vehicle on U.S. Highway 41 at approximately 4:40 am. Deputies joined in the pursuit and attempted a PIT maneuver on the vehicle. The suspect driver was able to regain control of his vehicle and continued to flee. Deputy Mecklenburg continued to pursuit the vehicle into Pasco County. His vehicle left the roadway and struck a tree near the intersection of Painter Road. His cruiser caught fire upon impact, but other officers in the pursuit were able to extinguish the flames. He was flown to a hospital in Tampa where he succumbed to his injuries. Another deputy was also injured earlier in the pursuit when his vehicle crashed. The suspect driver of the vehicle was arrested by members of the Florida Highway Patrol a short time later after being pursued through four counties. He was convicted and sentenced to life in prison. Deputy Mecklenburg was a U.S. Army veteran and had served with the Hernando County Sheriff's Office for two years. He is survived by his wife and two young children.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Vehicle pursuit Weapon: Automobile Offender: Apprehended; sentenced to life in prison

Constable Arthur Fleece Crenshaw Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Constable Arthur Fleece Crenshaw
honoree.summary: Constable Arthur Crenshaw and Federal Prohibition Agent John Waters, of the United States Treasury Department, were shot and killed while investigating illegal stills in Pasco County, Florida. They were shot from ambush as they sat in their car. Six men, five of them brothers, were arrested and charged with both murders. All were tried, but none were convicted. One was shot and killed on February 24th, 1925, by officers raiding a liquor still near the site where Constable Crenshaw and Agent Waters were killed. Constable Crenshaw was survived by his wife, son, mother, and brother. He is buried in Trilby Cemetery, Trilby, Pasco County, Florida.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Shotgun Offender: Apprehended

honoree.name: Bob Graham/Sunshine Skyway
honoree.summary: Bob Graham is an American politician and author born on November 9, 1936, in Coral Gables, Florida. He served as the 38th governor of Florida from 1979 to 1987 and as a United States senator from 1987 to 2005. Graham was a candidate for the Democratic nomination in the 2004 presidential election and was considered for vice president. He is known for his 'workdays' program and his work on intelligence oversight in the Senate.
honoree.education: University of Florida, Harvard Law School

honoree.name: Christa McAuliffe
honoree.summary: Christa McAuliffe was born on September 2, 1948, in Boston, Massachusetts. She was an American teacher and astronaut who was selected for NASA's Teacher in Space Project. McAuliffe was killed on January 28, 1986, when the Space Shuttle Challenger disintegrated shortly after launch. Her legacy endures as a symbol of education and space exploration.

honoree.name: Corporal James McWhorter
honoree.summary: Corporal James McWhorter was killed in a vehicle crash near the Agricultural Inspection Station on I-95 in Yulee, Florida. He was crossing from the northbound inspection station to the southbound inspection station when his vehicle was struck by an oncoming pickup truck at about 8:30 pm. All of the occupants of the truck suffered non-fatal injuries. Corporal McWhorter had served with the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services - Office of Agricultural Law Enforcement for 2-1/2 years. He is survived by his four children, parents, three brothers, two sisters, and fiancée. Corporal McWhorter was posthumously promoted to Corporal.
